messages.content:
Sub CopyDataFromXLSXInSameFolder()
    Dim sourceWorkbook As Workbook
    Dim destWorkbook As Workbook
    Dim sourceSheet As Worksheet
    Dim destSheet As Worksheet
    Dim folderPath As String
    Dim sourceFileName As String
    Dim sourceFilePath As String
    
    ' Dynamically get the folder path of the workbook containing this script (.xlsm)
    ' and ensure it ends with a backslash
    folderPath = ThisWorkbook.Path
    If Right(folderPath, 1) <> "\" Then
        folderPath = folderPath & "\"
    End If
    
    ' Get the name of the first .xlsx file in the folder
    sourceFileName = Dir(folderPath & "*.xlsx")

    ' Check if an .xlsx file was found
    If sourceFileName = "" Then
        MsgBox "No .xlsx file found in the same folder."
        Exit Sub
    End If
    
    ' Construct the full file path for the .xlsx file
    sourceFilePath = folderPath & sourceFileName
    
    ' Set the destination workbook and sheet
    ' ThisWorkbook refers to the workbook containing this script (.xlsm)
    Set destWorkbook = ThisWorkbook
    Set destSheet = destWorkbook.Sheets(1)  ' Adjust as needed if copying to a different sheet
    
    ' Attempt to open the source .xlsx file
    On Error Resume Next  ' In case the file doesn't open
    Set sourceWorkbook = Workbooks.Open(sourceFilePath)
    On Error GoTo 0  ' Turn back on regular error handling after attempt to open
    
    ' Check if the workbook was successfully opened
    If sourceWorkbook Is Nothing Then
        MsgBox "Failed to open the .xlsx file."
        Exit Sub
    End If
    
    ' Set the source sheet (assuming data is on the first sheet)
    Set sourceSheet = sourceWorkbook.Sheets(1)
    
    ' Copy the used range from the source sheet to the destination sheet
    sourceSheet.UsedRange.Copy Destination:=destSheet.Cells(1, 1)
    
    ' Close the source workbook without saving changes
    sourceWorkbook.Close SaveChanges:=False
    
    MsgBox "Data copied successfully from " & sourceFileName
End Sub
for this code i want to paste copied data in destination  starting with column b and row 4

To paste the copied data starting from column B (which is column 2) and row 4 in the destination sheet, you need to modify the destination parameter in the `.Copy` method. Here's the adjusted part of your code to achieve this:

Replace the line:

```vba
sourceSheet.UsedRange.Copy Destination:=destSheet.Cells(1, 1)
```

with:

```vba
sourceSheet.UsedRange.Copy Destination:=destSheet.Cells(4, 2) ' Starts pasting from B4
```

This sets the top-left cell of the paste area to B4. The rest of the used range from the source sheet will be pasted starting from this cell, effectively placing the data beginning at column B and row 4 in the destination sheet.

Your entire revised function would look like this:

```vba
Sub CopyDataFromXLSXInSameFolder()
    Dim sourceWorkbook As Workbook
    Dim destWorkbook As Workbook
    Dim sourceSheet As Worksheet
    Dim destSheet As Worksheet
    Dim folderPath As String
    Dim sourceFileName As String
    Dim sourceFilePath As String
    
    ' Dynamically get the folder path of the workbook containing this script (.xlsm)
    ' and ensure it ends with a backslash
    folderPath = ThisWorkbook.Path
    If Right(folderPath, 1) <> "\" Then
        folderPath = folderPath & "\"
    End If
    
    ' Get the name of the first .xlsx file in the folder
    sourceFileName = Dir(folderPath & "*.xlsx")

    ' Check if an .xlsx file was found
    If sourceFileName = "" Then
        MsgBox "No .xlsx file found in the same folder."
        Exit Sub
    End If
    
    ' Construct the full file path for the .xlsx file
    sourceFilePath = folderPath & sourceFileName
    
    ' Set the destination workbook and sheet
    ' ThisWorkbook refers to the workbook containing this script (.xlsm)
    Set destWorkbook = ThisWorkbook
    Set destSheet = destWorkbook.Sheets(1)  ' Adjust as needed if copying to a different sheet
    
    ' Attempt to open the source .xlsx file
    On Error Resume Next  ' In case the file doesn't open
    Set sourceWorkbook = Workbooks.Open(sourceFilePath)
    On Error GoTo 0  ' Turn back on regular error handling after attempt to open
    
    ' Check if the workbook was successfully opened
    If sourceWorkbook Is Nothing Then
        MsgBox "Failed to open the .xlsx file."
        Exit Sub
    End If
    
    ' Set the source sheet (assuming data is on the first sheet)
    Set sourceSheet = sourceWorkbook.Sheets(1)
    
    ' Copy the used range from the source sheet to the destination sheet
    sourceSheet.UsedRange.Copy Destination:=destSheet.Cells(4, 2) ' Starts pasting from B4
    
    ' Close the source workbook without saving changes
    sourceWorkbook.Close SaveChanges:=False
    
    MsgBox "Data copied successfully from " & sourceFileName
End Sub
```

This code alteration ensures that the data is pasted into your destination workbook starting from the specified location. Always ensure that your destination range has enough space to accommodate the data being copied to avoid overwriting any important data unintentionally.
for %i in (*.pdf) do gswin64c -q -dNOPAUSE -sDEVICE=txtwrite -sOutputFile=“%~ni.txt” -dFirstPage=1 -dLastPage=1 “%i” -c quit    for this i want to make in excel if i click one button to run

To execute a command similar to the one you provided (for converting the first page of PDF files to text) from within Excel VBA when clicking a button, you can follow these steps. This method involves using VBA to call the command line and process each PDF file in a specified directory:

1. **Insert a Form Control Button**:
    - Go to your Excel sheet.
    - Select "Developer" in the ribbon. If you don't see the Developer tab, you'll first need to enable it from Excel Options.
    - Click on "Insert" and under "Form Controls", choose the Button control.
    - Draw the button on your sheet.
    - Assign a new macro to it and click "New". You'll be taken to the VBA editor.

2. **VBA Code to Process PDFs**:
    - Replace the template code within your new macro with something like the following. Be sure to adjust the command path for Ghostscript (`gswin64c`) and the folder path where your PDF files are located.

```vba
Sub Button1_Click()
    Dim gsPath As String
    Dim folderPath As String
    Dim cmdCommand As String
    Dim fileName As String
    Dim fs, folder, file, files As Object
    
    ' Path to Ghostscript EXE (adjust it to your Ghostscript installation)
    gsPath = "C:\Program Files\gs\gs9.54.0\bin\gswin64c.exe"
    
    ' Folder with PDF files (adjust to your needs)
    folderPath = "C:\Your\Path\Here\"
    
    ' Create FileSystemObject
    Set fs = CreateObject("Scripting.FileSystemObject")
    Set folder = fs.GetFolder(folderPath)
    Set files = folder.Files

    ' Loop through all PDF files in the folder
    For Each file In files
        If LCase(fs.GetExtensionName(file)) = "pdf" Then
            fileName = fs.GetBaseName(file)
            
            ' Command to convert PDF to txt (for the first page only)
            cmdCommand = """" & gsPath & """ -q -dNOPAUSE -sDEVICE=txtwrite" & _
                         " -sOutputFile=""" & folderPath & fileName & ".txt""" & _
                         " -dFirstPage=1 -dLastPage=1 """ & file.Path & """ -c quit"
            
            ' Execute the command
            Call Shell("cmd.exe /S /C " & cmdCommand, vbNormalFocus)
        End If
    Next file
    
    MsgBox "Conversion complete!"
End Sub
```
Make sure you adjust the following according to your setup:

- `gsPath`: This should point to the executable of Ghostscript on your computer. You might need to adjust the path.
- `folderPath`: This is where your PDF files are located and where the output text files will be saved.

3. **Permissions**:
    - Note that running external applications and scripts via VBA requires permissions; ensure your macro settings allow this.
    - Your antivirus or Windows may block scripts from running or require additional permissions.

4. **Running the Macro**:
    - Once the code is in place, simply go back to your Excel sheet and click the button you've added. It will process all PDF files in the specified directory, converting the first page of each to a text file via the Ghostscript `txtwrite` device.

Remember, your Excel and system security settings need to allow for this operation, and paths in the script must be correctly set for your environment.
i dont want to specify folder , i active opended folder where pdf are

If you want your VBA script to use the currently active folder path from which you've opened the Excel file and process PDF files within that directory, you can use `ThisWorkbook.Path` to dynamically obtain the path. Below is a revised version of the script that does exactly that:

```vba
Sub ConvertPDFsToText()
    Dim gsPath As String
    Dim folderPath As String
    Dim cmdCommand As String
    Dim fileName As String
    Dim fs, file, files As Object
    
    ' Path to Ghostscript EXE (adjust it to your Ghostscript installation)
    gsPath = "C:\Program Files\gs\gs9.54.0\bin\gswin64c.exe"
    
    ' Get the folder path where the current Excel workbook is located
    folderPath = ThisWorkbook.Path & "\"
    
    ' Create FileSystemObject
    Set fs = CreateObject("Scripting.FileSystemObject")
    Set files = fs.GetFolder(folderPath).Files
    
    ' Loop through all PDF files in the folder
    For Each file In files
        If LCase(fs.GetExtensionName(file)) = "pdf" Then
            fileName = fs.GetBaseName(file)
            
            ' Command to convert PDF to txt (for the first page only)
            cmdCommand = """" & gsPath & """ -q -dNOPAUSE -sDEVICE=txtwrite" & _
                         " -sOutputFile=""" & folderPath & fileName & ".txt""" & _
                         " -dFirstPage=1 -dLastPage=1 """ & file.Path & """ -c quit"
            
            ' Execute the command
            Call Shell("cmd.exe /S /C " & cmdCommand, vbNormalFocus)
        End If
    Next file
    
    MsgBox "Conversion complete!"
End Sub
```

This script will:
1. Obtain the path of the folder from which the active Excel workbook was opened (`ThisWorkbook.Path`).
2. Loop through all files in this folder.
3. Check for files with the `.pdf` extension.
4. Use Ghostscript to convert the first page of each PDF file to a text file, saving it with the same name as the original PDF but with a `.txt` extension in the same folder.

Some points to note:

- Ensure that the path to the Ghostscript executable (`gsPath`) is correctly specified for your system.
- This script assumes that the user has the necessary permissions to execute external commands through VBA and that Ghostscript is correctly installed.
- Depending on your Macro Security Settings, you may need to adjust them to allow this script to run.
- It's always a good idea to back up your files before running batch operations on them.

By using `ThisWorkbook.Path`, the script dynamically works with the folder of the currently opened Excel workbook, removing the need to hardcode the folder path.
OK, bitdefender finthis as a virus

When running scripts or macros that execute system commands or interact with external applications (like Ghostscript in your case), it's possible that antivirus software, such as Bitdefender, may flag these actions as suspicious or potentially harmful. This is because malware often uses similar techniques to perform unauthorized actions on a computer.

### Here are a few steps you can take to address this issue:

1. **Whitelist the Application or Script:**
   - Most antivirus software, including Bitdefender, allows you to whitelist (exclude) certain programs or scripts from being scanned. You can add your Excel file, the folder containing your files, or the Ghostscript executable to the antivirus exclusion list. Remember, this should be done with caution and only if you're sure the files and applications are safe.

2. **Review Antivirus Alert:**
   - Carefully review the alert provided by Bitdefender. Sometimes, the alert details can give you insights into why the action was flagged and suggest how to mitigate the issue without compromising security.

3. **Ensure Software Up-to-Date:**
   - Make sure that both your antivirus software and Ghostscript are up to date. Sometimes, issues arise from using older versions of software that might have known vulnerabilities or compatibility issues that can cause false positives in antivirus scans.

4. **Contact Support:**
   - If you're using Bitdefender for a business, you may have access to customer support. Contacting Bitdefender support can provide guidance specific to your situation, helping you to safely run your script without compromising your antivirus protection.

5. **Use Antivirus Safe Practices:**
   - Ensure your macro or script follows safe coding practices. Avoid actions that could genuinely harm your computer or data, such as disabling security features or executing untrusted code.

6. **Manual Conversion as Alternative:**
   - If your attempts to use Ghostscript through Excel VBA continue to be blocked, consider manually converting your PDFs using Ghostscript commands in a command prompt window that you've explicitly opened as an administrator. This approach is more visible and controllable but less convenient for batch processing.

7. **Verify Script Integrity:**
   - Ensure that your script does exactly what it's supposed to do, with no hidden actions. Sometimes, ensuring transparency of the script's actions can help determine if an exception should be made.

### Final Note
It's paramount to balance security with functionality. While it's tempting to disable antivirus checks for certain tasks, ensure that you fully trust the applications and scripts involved. Security software flags are generally geared towards protecting your system, and each exception made should be considered carefully.

manually converting your PDFs using Ghostscript commands in a command prompt window that you’ve explicitly opened as an administrator. This approach is more visible and controllable but less convenient for batch processing.

Manually converting PDFs to text or another format using Ghostscript commands in a command prompt requires you to follow a few steps. This approach will give you direct control over the conversion process and enable you to see exactly what happens at each step. Here's how you can do it:

### 1. Open Command Prompt as Administrator

- **Windows 10/11:** Right-click the Start button, select "Windows Terminal (Admin)" or "Command Prompt (Admin)" from the menu that appears. If you don't see Command Prompt directly, you might find it under "Windows Tools" or by searching for it in the start menu.

### 2. Navigate to Your PDF Files' Directory

- Use the `cd` command to change the directory to where your PDF files are located. For example, if your PDFs are in "C:\Documents\PDFs", you would enter:
  ```cmd
  cd C:\Documents\PDFs
  ```

### 3. Run Ghostscript Command for Conversion

- Use the following Ghostscript command template to convert a PDF to text. Replace `<input.pdf>` with your PDF file's name and `<output.txt>` with your desired output text file's name.
  
  For Windows:
  ```cmd
  gswin64c -sDEVICE=txtwrite -o output.txt input.pdf
  ```

- If you’re converting only the first page, add `-dFirstPage=1 -dLastPage=1` to the command:
  ```cmd
  gswin64c -dNOPAUSE -sDEVICE=txtwrite -o output.txt -dFirstPage=1 -dLastPage=1 input.pdf
  ```

- Ensure you replace `gswin64c` with `gswin32c` if you are on a 32-bit system or if that's the Ghostscript version you have installed.

### 4. Repeat for Multiple PDFs

- To process multiple PDFs, you have to repeat the command for each file, changing `<input.pdf>` and `<output.txt>` each time to the current file you're converting.

### Example:
If you have a file named "example.pdf" and you want to convert its first page to text, your command would look like this:

```cmd
gswin64c -dNOPAUSE -sDEVICE=txtwrite -o example.txt -dFirstPage=1 -dLastPage=1 example.pdf
```

### Note:
- This manual method involves running a command for each PDF file you wish to convert, which can be time-consuming for large numbers of files.
- Scripts and batch processing techniques previously discussed would automate this, but if security software restricts script execution, manual conversion becomes a safer, though more labor-intensive, alternative.
- Ensure Ghostscript is installed on your system and its path is included in the system's environment variables for the `gswin64c` command to be recognized in any directory via the command prompt.
for %i in (*.pdf) do gswin64c -q -dNOPAUSE -sDEVICE=txtwrite -sOutputFile=“%~ni.txt” -dFirstPage=1 -dLastPage=1 “%i” -c quit, can i make a  shortcut for this command to only just click on them ?

Yes, you can create a shortcut that will execute this command with just a click. Doing so on a Windows system involves creating a batch file and then optionally creating a shortcut to that batch file for even easier access. Here's how you can do it:

### Step 1: Create a Batch File

1. **Open Notepad** or any plain text editor.
2. **Copy and paste** the following code into it. Make sure to replace the quotation marks (“ ”) with straight quotes (""), as curved quotes may cause issues in scripts:
   ```batch
   for %%i in (*.pdf) do gswin64c -q -dNOPAUSE -sDEVICE=txtwrite -sOutputFile="%%~ni.txt" -dFirstPage=1 -dLastPage=1 "%%i" -c quit
   ```
3. **Save** the file with a `.bat` extension, for example, `ConvertPDFsToText.bat`. Ensure you select "All Files" in the "Save as type" field in Notepad to avoid saving it as a text file with a `.txt` extension.

### Step 2: Create a Shortcut (Optional)

1. Right-click on the `.bat` file you just created and select **Create shortcut**. If you're prompted that shortcuts cannot be created here and if you'd like to place the shortcut on the desktop instead, choose **Yes**.
2. **Right-click** on the new shortcut and select **Properties**.
3. In the **Shortcut** tab, you can further customize how the script runs:
   - **Start in**: You might want to specify the folder where your PDFs are. If they're always in the same folder as the batch file, leave this empty.
   - **Run**: You can choose to run the window minimized to reduce disruption.
4. Click **OK** to save your changes.

### Step 3: Use the Shortcut/Batch File

- **With the Batch File**: Double-click the `.bat` file. It will open a Command Prompt window and execute your command on all PDF files in its directory.
- **With the Shortcut**: Double-click the shortcut to do the same as above. If you've set it to run minimized, you'll see less activity on your screen.

### Additional Customization

- If you need this script to run in a specific directory, you can modify the batch file to first change the directory. Add this line before the `for` command, adjusting the path to your target directory:
   ```batch
   cd /d "C:\Path\To\Your\PDFs"
   ```
- Ensure Ghostscript is installed and `gswin64c` is accessible globally; otherwise, you might need to provide the full path to `gswin64c` in the batch file.

Creating a shortcut to a batch file like this can greatly simplify repetitive tasks, but remember to use caution when running batch files, especially those downloaded from the Internet or received via email, as they can execute harmful commands if not properly vetted.
unrecovereabe error

If you encountered an "unrecoverable error" while trying to execute your batch file or Ghostscript command, this could be due to a few different issues. Here are some steps to troubleshoot and resolve common causes of such errors:

### 1. Incorrect Ghostscript Command or Syntax Error

- **Syntax**: Verify that the command syntax in your batch file is correct. Pay particular attention to quotation marks, spaces, and other special characters. Instead of using “curved quotes”, use "straight quotes".
- **Command Path**: Ensure the command path to `gswin64c` (Ghostscript) is correct. If Ghostscript is not in your system's PATH environment variable, you may need to specify the full path to `gswin64c` in your batch script.

### 2. Verify Ghostscript Installation

- Ensure Ghostscript is properly installed on your machine. Try running `gswin64c -version` in a command prompt to check if it returns the version information. If not, reinstall Ghostscript or correct your system's PATH variable to include the directory where Ghostscript is installed.

### 3. File Access or Permissions Issues

- **File Access**: Make sure the PDF files you are trying to convert are not open in another program. Locked files can cause the script to fail.
- **Permissions**: Check that you have read/write access to both the PDF files you're converting and the folder where you're trying to save the output `.txt` files.

### 4. Check for Corrupted PDF Files

- A corrupted PDF file might cause Ghostscript to encounter an error. Try running the command on a different PDF file that you know is valid. If it works with other files, the original PDF may be corrupted.

### 5. Ghostscript Version Compatibility

- Verify that the version of Ghostscript you have installed supports the commands and features you're using. If necessary, update Ghostscript to the latest version.

### 6. Running from the Correct Directory

- Ensure your batch file is located in the same directory as the PDF files you're trying to convert, or modify the script to navigate (`cd`) to the correct directory first.

### Example of a Corrected Batch File with Full Path to Ghostscript

Here's an example showing how to include the full path to `gswin64c` in your batch file:

```batch
@echo off
cd /d "C:\Path\To\Your\PDFs"
for %%i in (*.pdf) do "C:\Program Files\gs\gs9.xx\bin\gswin64c.exe" -q -dNOPAUSE -sDEVICE=txtwrite -sOutputFile="%%~ni.txt" -dFirstPage=1 -dLastPage=1 "%%i" -c quit
echo Conversion Complete!
pause
```
Replace `C:\Program Files\gs\gs9.xx\bin\gswin64c.exe` with the actual path to your `gswin64c.exe` file, and `C:\Path\To\Your\PDFs` with the path to your PDF files.

### 7. Run as Administrator

- Right-click the batch file and select "Run as administrator" to ensure it has the necessary permissions.

If you've tried these steps and continue to encounter problems, consider reviewing the error message for specific details or consulting the official Ghostscript documentation and forums for guidance on the error.

If you need the batch file to work with PDF files in the active folder from which the batch file is run (without having to specify the folder path), you can simplify the batch file. This approach makes the batch file more versatile, as it will work with PDF files in whatever folder the batch file is placed and executed, avoiding the need for hard-coded paths.

Here is the revised batch script without a specified path, ensuring it operates on PDF files in the current (or "active") directory:

```batch
@echo off
for %%i in (*.pdf) do gswin64c -q -dNOPAUSE -sDEVICE=txtwrite -sOutputFile="%%~ni.txt" -dFirstPage=1 -dLastPage=1 "%%i" -c quit
echo Conversion Complete!
pause
```

Follow these steps to use this script:

1. **Save the above script** in a text file with a `.bat` extension, for example, `ConvertPDFs.bat`.

2. **Place the batch file** in the same folder as the PDF files you wish to convert.

3. **Double-click** the batch file to run it. The script will process all PDF files in the folder, converting the first page of each to a text file with the same base name.

4. **Running without Pausing**: If you prefer the Command Prompt window to close automatically after the script runs (without pressing any key), remove the `pause` command from the script.

This script does not require modifying the `cd` command to navigate to a specific directory since it executes in the directory from which it is run, using the relative paths to the PDF files found there.

If you encounter permissions issues or errors indicating Ghostscript can't be found, ensure:

- Ghostscript is correctly installed, and its path is correctly set in your system if you're not specifying the full path in the script.
- You have necessary permissions to read the PDF files and write to the folder.

Also, if you're running this from a network drive or a location with restricted permissions, consider copying the PDFs and the batch file to a local hard drive to test if this resolves the issue.

If you want the path for `gswin64c` to be fully specified in your batch file script to ensure that Ghostscript is correctly found regardless of the system's environment variables, you can do so by including the full path to the `gswin64c` executable within the script. Here’s how you can modify the batch file to include the full path to Ghostscript:

```batch
@echo off
for %%i in (*.pdf) do "C:\Path\To\Ghostscript\bin\gswin64c.exe" -q -dNOPAUSE -sDEVICE=txtwrite -sOutputFile="%%~ni.txt" -dFirstPage=1 -dLastPage=1 "%%i" -c quit
echo Conversion Complete!
pause
```
Replace `C:\Path\To\Ghostscript\bin\gswin64c.exe` with the actual full path to your `gswin64c.exe`. The path depends on where Ghostscript is installed on your system. For example, if you installed Ghostscript version 9.54, the default installation path might be something like `C:\Program Files\gs\gs9.54.0\bin\gswin64c.exe`, but you need to verify the path on your system.

### Steps to find the full path of Ghostscript:

1. **Navigate to the Ghostscript installation directory**: This is typically located in `C:\Program Files\gs` or `C:\Program Files (x86)\gs`, depending on your version and architecture.

2. **Find the `bin` directory**: Inside the Ghostscript installation directory, look for the `bin` folder.

3. **Locate `gswin64c.exe`**: Inside the `bin` folder, find the `gswin64c.exe` executable. The path to this file is what you need.

4. **Copy the path**: Click on the address bar in File Explorer to reveal the full path, add `\gswin64c.exe` to the end, and copy it.

You can then use this path in your batch file to ensure Ghostscript is always correctly executed, regardless of the environment variables on the computer where the batch file runs.

Remember to test the batch file in a directory with PDFs to ensure everything works as expected.

what is wrong with this cose ?

The primary issue with the code snippet you've posted seems to arise from the use of “curly” or “smart” quotes instead of the standard "straight" quotes that command line and batch scripts require. Additionally, there's an extra backslash in the Ghostscript executable path that shouldn't be there. Here's how you can correct it:

```batch
@echo off
SETLOCAL
for %%i in (*.pdf) do "C:\Program Files (x86)\gs\gs10.03.0\bin\gswin32c.exe" -q -dNOPAUSE -sDEVICE=txtwrite -sOutputFile="%%~ni.txt" -dFirstPage=1 -dLastPage=1 "%%i" -c quit
echo Conversion Complete!
ENDLOCAL
pause
```

Corrections made:

1. **Straight Quotes**: Replaced “curly quotes” with "straight quotes".
2. **Path to gswin32c.exe**: Removed the trailing backslash after `gswin32c.exe` in the path. It's crucial when specifying file paths to get these details correct, as an incorrect path will cause the command to fail.

Ensure that:

- The path to Ghostscript (`gswin32c.exe`) provided matches the actual installation path on your computer. The version `gs10.03.0` you mentioned should correspond with the Ghostscript version you've installed. If it differs, adjust the path accordingly.
- You're using `gswin32c.exe` since you're pointing to the "Program Files (x86)" directory, which implies a 32-bit application on a 64-bit system. If you're running a 64-bit version of Ghostscript on a 64-bit system, you might be using `gswin64c.exe` and the path would likely be `"C:\Program Files\gs\...`.

how can i bacht convert pdf files to txt ? are any free progran taht do that ?

Yes, there are multiple ways and tools available to batch convert PDF files to text for free. These range from command-line utilities like Ghostscript and Xpdf to graphical interfaces and online tools. Here's an overview of some options:

### 1. **Ghostscript**

You've already been looking into using Ghostscript. It's a powerful tool that, among other capabilities, can convert PDF files to text. The correct approach would be similar to what has been discussed: using the `txtwrite` device to process the files. Ensure your syntax is correct and that you're running it in an environment where Ghostscript is installed and accessible.

### 2. **Xpdf**

Xpdf is another free toolset that includes `pdftotext`, a command-line utility to convert PDF files to plain text.

- **Xpdf Installation**: First, you'll need to download and install Xpdf from their official website.
- **Using pdftotext**: The basic usage of `pdftotext` is straightforward. In a command prompt or terminal, you would use:
  ```sh
  pdftotext input.pdf output.txt
  ```
- **Batch Processing**: For batch processing, you can use a loop in a batch script (Windows) or a shell script (Linux/Mac), similar to the Ghostscript example.

### 3. **Calibre**

Calibre is a free, open-source ebook manager that includes comprehensive file conversion capabilities, including PDF to TXT.

- You can add multiple PDF files to Calibre and convert them in bulk to TXT or other formats.
- The graphical interface makes it easy to use for those uncomfortable with command-line tools.

### 4. **Online Services**

Several online services offer free PDF to text conversion. Websites like `Zamzar`, `PDF2Text`, and `Smallpdf` provide an interface to upload your PDFs and download the resulting text files. However, consider the privacy and security implications when using these services, especially with sensitive documents.

### Choosing Between Offline and Online Tools

- **Privacy**: If the PDFs contain sensitive information, offline tools (like Ghostscript, Xpdf, or Calibre) are usually safer because your files don't leave your computer.
- **Convenience**: Online tools can be more convenient for quick conversions or when you're not able to install software, but they often have limitations in terms of file size or the number of conversions.

Each of these tools has its strengths, and the best choice depends on your specific requirements, including the workflow, the volume of material to convert, and concerns about data privacy.

The error message you're seeing, "The filename, directory name, or volume label syntax is incorrect," often occurs due to syntax issues in the command. Based on your script, the issue most likely arises from the use of special “curly” quotation marks (“ ”) rather than the standard "straight" quotation marks ("). Additionally, ensure there are no extra characters or spaces that don't belong, especially near the executable path and the options you've specified.

Here's a corrected version of your script:

```batch
@echo off
SETLOCAL
for %%i in (*.pdf) do "C:\Program Files (x86)\gs\gs10.03.0\bin\gswin32c.exe" -q -dNOPAUSE -sDEVICE=txtwrite -sOutputFile="%%~ni.txt" -dFirstPage=1 -dLastPage=1 "%%i" -c quit
echo Conversion Complete!
ENDLOCAL
pause
```
Make sure of the following:

1. **Straight Quotes:** Replace all “curly” quotation marks with standard "straight" quotation marks.
2. **Correct Path:** Check that the path to `gswin32c.exe` is correct. The path provided should exactly match where Ghostscript is installed on your computer. If you've installed a different version or if Ghostscript is in a different directory, adjust the path accordingly.
3. **Syntax:** Ensure there are no trailing backslashes or unwanted characters in the path to `gswin32c.exe`.

To fix these issues, copy the corrected script into a new text file and save it with a `.bat` extension. Then, run this `.bat` file in a directory where your PDF files are located. 

If you continue to experience issues, check the following:

- **Ghostscript Installation:** Ensure Ghostscript is installed correctly and that you can run `gswin32c` from the command line. You might test this by opening a CMD window, typing the full path to `gswin32c.exe` followed by `-version`, and seeing if it outputs the version information.
  
  Example:
  ```cmd
  "C:\Program Files (x86)\gs\gs10.03.0\bin\gswin32c.exe" -version
  ```

- **Admin Rights:** Some directories, especially within `Program Files`, might require administrative privileges to write files. Try running the batch script as an administrator.

- **Batch File Location:** Ensure the batch file is located in a directory with PDF files you want to convert, or adjust the script to point to the specific directory where your PDF files are stored.
